---
title: "synpmx"
subtitle: "Generating structurally faithful synthetic pharmacometric datasets"
date: 2026-07-23
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

## Contents

- Background and context
- The shared philosophy: `synadam` and `synpmx`
- How does `synpmx` work?
- Example output
- Safeguards against data mix-up

# Background and context

## The problem

- Real pharmacometric (PMX) modeling data can only be accessed in restricted
  (GxP) environments — for us, GPS-II.
- Modelers want to build and debug analysis code — cleaning, joins, reshaping,
  plotting, control-file plumbing, repeated-dose and longitudinal workflows — on
  their **local machines**, where richer tooling and coding agents are
  available.
- A NONMEM-style event table (ID/TIME/DV/AMT/EVID/…) is structurally far richer
  than a flat analysis dataset: dosing records, observation records, multiple
  endpoints, censoring, and per-subject timing all have to be *coherent*, not
  just present.
- `synpmx` generates synthetic PMX datasets — **structurally exact, vaguely
  right on the numbers** — that can be **moved out of GPS-II to a local
  machine**, developed against there, then run unchanged back on the real data.

## The accuracy bar, stated plainly

> **Structure must be right. Numbers need only be in the right region.**

A user should be able to develop and debug an analysis pipeline against the
synthetic data and have it run **unchanged** against the real data — while being
unable to draw any scientific conclusion from it.

The deliberate lack of statistical fidelity is a **privacy advantage**, not a
limitation to be engineered away.

# The shared philosophy

## `synadam` and `synpmx` are the same idea

The package **`synadam`** generates mock ADaM datasets by **resampling each
*column*** from real data:

- continuous → uniform draw over the observed range;
- categorical → resample the observed categories;
- flags → sampled in the observed proportions.

No modeling, no elicitation. It relies on **governance** — ADaM data is
already analysis-level de-identified and stays inside a controlled environment —
rather than on a mathematical guarantee. This is standard, accepted practice.

## Same philosophy, different granularity

| | `synadam` | `synpmx` |
|---|---|---|
| Unit resampled | a **column** | a **subject trajectory** |
| Preserves | marginal support | joint structure, timing, shape |
| Privacy model | governance | governance |
| Data shape | flat ADaM table | NONMEM-style event table |

**`synpmx` is `synadam`'s philosophy lifted from the column to the whole subject
trajectory** — because a PMX event table only makes sense as a coherent whole.

# How does synpmx work?

## Blend whole subject trajectories

`synpmx_avatar()`. For each generated subject:

1. **Sample** a compatible source subject's event skeleton as a template
   (its dosing/observation structure and timing).
2. **Blend** covariates and endpoint trajectories from a distance-weighted set
   of a few similar source subjects.
3. **Perturb** with subject-level and within-trajectory noise.

The output looks like real data because it is **built from real data** — whole
real subjects come through, blended, so real covariate correlations and
trajectory shapes are preserved for free.

## Why blend at the trajectory level

A PMX event table only makes sense as a **coherent whole**: dosing records,
observation records, per-subject timing, and multiple endpoints all have to line
up. Resampling column by column would break that coherence.

- **Works at any cohort size** — twelve subjects is enough.
- **No elicitation** — no model, no priors, no protocol declaration. Point it at
  the data and go.
- **Structurally exact, vaguely right on the numbers** — exactly the accuracy
  bar the workflow needs.

## Where the synthetic data goes

The whole point is to **move the synthetic data**: out of the restricted GPS-II
environment and onto a managed local machine, so modelers can work with modern tooling and coding agents.

And it is **only synthetic data that moves.** Real subject-level data never
leaves GPS-II.

# Example output

## Configure column roles, then synthesize

```{r}
library(synpmx)

# Map your columns onto PMX roles (the only thing you edit)
roles <- pmx_roles(
  id = "ID", time = "TIME", dv = "DV",
  amt = "AMT", evid = "EVID", dvid = "DVID",
  cmt = "CMT", mdv = "MDV",
  covariates = c("WT", "AGE", "SEX")
)

# Validate the source structure, then blend
report    <- validate_pmx(raw, roles, strict = FALSE)
synthetic <- synpmx_avatar(raw, roles, n_subjects = NULL, seed = 4127)
```

No model, no priors — just the column meanings.

## Source vs synthetic (diagnostic)

A concentration-time scatter of source against synthetic shows the synthetic
cohort reproducing the **shape, timing, and magnitude** of the real profiles
without reproducing any individual subject.

# Safeguards against data mix-up

## Keeping synthetic and real data apart

- **New identifiers.** Synthetic subject IDs never overlap source IDs
  (checked: `intersect(synthetic$ID, raw$ID)` is empty).
- **Separate storage.** Synthetic tables are written to a dedicated `output/`
  directory, never alongside source datasets.
- **Only synthetic data leaves GPS-II.** The move to a local machine carries the
  synthetic table alone; real subject-level data stays in the restricted
  environment.
- **Explicit scope labeling.** The synthetic output is marked as structurally
  faithful but not statistically faithful — for pipeline development, not
  analysis.

## Summary

- `synpmx` extends the **`synadam` philosophy** — resample real data, rely on
  governance — from the **column** to the **subject trajectory**, because a PMX
  event table is only coherent as a whole.
- It **blends whole real subject trajectories**: structurally exact, works at
  any N, needs no elicitation.
- The payoff: **move synthetic PMX data out of GPS-II to a local machine** —
  still inside Novartis's trusted boundary — and develop against it with modern
  tooling, then run unchanged on the real data.
